title: How to Buy Commercial Property in Australia: What the Agents Won't Tell You
excerpt: Commercial property buying is fundamentally different from residential. The due diligence is harder, the risks are less obvious, and the selling agent is not on your side. Here's what you need to know.
author: Joe Kelley
metaTitle: How to Buy Commercial Property in Australia | Your Office Space
metaDescription: What residential buyers get wrong about commercial property. Due diligence checklist, common traps, and what a commercial buyers agent actually does for you.
body:
Buying commercial property in Australia is not like buying residential. The returns can be better, the leases longer, and the yield more reliable — but the risks are also less obvious, and the market is significantly less transparent.

Here's what experienced buyers know that first-timers often learn the hard way.

## The selling agent is not on your side

This is the most important thing to understand. The selling agent — the person showing you through the property, answering your questions, sending you the IM — is retained by and paid by the vendor. Their job is to achieve the highest possible price for their client.

They are not neutral. They are not your adviser. They are not there to highlight the risks.

A commercial buyers agent works exclusively for you. That's the fundamental difference.

## Yield vs capital growth

Commercial property is typically valued on yield — not comparable sales the way residential is. The income the property generates, divided by the purchase price, gives you the yield. Cap rates in Australia currently range from around 5% in premium CBD assets to 7–9% in regional and secondary markets.

The risk: if the tenant leaves and the property becomes vacant, the yield goes to zero — and so does the value, dramatically. Understanding tenant covenant strength (the financial quality and lease security of the tenant) is critical before you buy.

## Lease structures — what you need to check

- **Lease expiry:** When does the lease end? Anything less than 3 years of term remaining is a short-WALE (Weighted Average Lease Expiry) risk.
- **Options:** Does the tenant have options to renew? Exercised options are security; unexercised options are not.
- **Rent review mechanism:** Fixed percentage, CPI, or market review — see our leasing guide for why this matters.
- **Outgoings:** Net, gross, or modified gross? Who pays what?
- **Make-good obligations:** What is the tenant required to do on exit?
- **Assignment and subletting:** Can the tenant sublet? Transfer the lease? Under what conditions?

## Due diligence checklist

Before exchanging contracts on a commercial property, you should have reviewed or obtained:

- Full title search (encumbrances, caveats, easements)
- Current lease and all variations or side agreements
- Rent roll and outgoings history (3 years)
- Building condition report (structural, roof, services)
- Environmental and contamination assessment (for industrial)
- Zoning and planning certificates — permitted use confirmed for current and intended use
- Strata plan and levies (if applicable)
- Insurance schedule
- GST position — going concern exemption if applicable

## The off-market advantage

The best commercial properties rarely hit the portals. Agents sell them quietly — to known buyers, through their network, before they bother listing.

A buyers agent with active relationships in the local commercial market gets calls before the listing goes live. That access matters. You're not competing with ten other buyers who saw the same portal listing.
